title: VPN DNS Leak Test: What It Is, How to Test, and How to Fix It
description: A DNS leak means your ISP can see which sites you visit even when your VPN is active. This guide explains what causes DNS leaks, how to test for them, and how to fix them.
content:
## What Is a DNS Leak?

When you type a website address into your browser, your device has to translate that domain name into an IP address. This translation request is called a DNS query, and it goes to a DNS server. Under normal circumstances (no VPN), that request goes to your ISP's DNS server, which means your ISP has a complete record of every domain you visit.

When you connect to a VPN, DNS queries are supposed to go through the VPN's encrypted tunnel to the VPN's own DNS servers. Your ISP should see only encrypted traffic going to a VPN server and nothing about your destination.

A DNS leak happens when DNS queries bypass the VPN tunnel and reach your ISP's DNS server anyway. Your traffic content may be encrypted, but your ISP (or anyone monitoring that DNS server) can still see every domain you look up. The VPN tunnel protects the payload but not the envelope.

## Why DNS Leaks Happen

Several technical conditions can cause DNS queries to escape the tunnel:

**Windows Smart Multi-Homed Name Resolution.** Windows 8 and later introduced a feature that sends DNS queries to all available network interfaces simultaneously and uses whichever responds fastest. When a VPN is active, Windows can still send queries out through the physical network adapter. Most quality VPNs disable this, but misconfigured VPNs or OS-level overrides can leave it active.

**IPv6 without IPv6 leak protection.** Most VPNs handle IPv4 traffic correctly. If your network uses IPv6 and your VPN doesn't handle IPv6 DNS, those requests can bypass the tunnel entirely and go to your ISP's IPv6 DNS server.

**Static DNS entries.** If you have manually set a DNS server in your network adapter settings (a common practice, for example to use Google's 8.8.8.8 or Cloudflare's 1.1.1.1), those static entries can persist and be used even when the VPN is active.

**DHCP override.** Some routers push DNS server settings via DHCP that override VPN configuration. This is more common on corporate networks and some ISP-provided routers.

**Outdated or poorly configured VPN apps.** Older VPN clients or misconfigured setups may not properly route DNS through the tunnel.

## How to Test for DNS Leaks

Testing takes about two minutes.

**Step 1: Establish a baseline.** Disconnect your VPN completely. Go to [dnsleaktest.com](https://www.dnsleaktest.com) or [browserleaks.com/dns](https://browserleaks.com/dns). Click the standard or extended test. You should see your ISP's DNS servers listed. Note your real IP address and which DNS provider appears.

**Step 2: Connect to your VPN.** Connect to any VPN server and run the same test again. This time, the DNS servers shown should belong to your VPN provider and be located in the country of the server you connected to.

**If you see your ISP's DNS servers while connected to the VPN, you have a DNS leak.**

Other reliable test tools: [ipleak.net](https://ipleak.net) (also shows WebRTC and IPv6), [vpntesting.com/dns-leak-test](https://vpntesting.com/dns-leak-test), and NordVPN's own leak test at nordvpn.com/dns-leak-test.

## How to Fix a DNS Leak

### Fix 1: Use a VPN With Built-In DNS Leak Protection

The easiest fix is to use a VPN that handles this automatically. NordVPN, ProtonVPN, Surfshark, and ExpressVPN all run their own encrypted DNS servers and force DNS queries through the tunnel. NordVPN additionally blocks queries to any DNS server other than their own when the VPN is active.

If you're already using one of these and still seeing a leak, the issue is likely a configuration problem on your device, not the VPN itself.

### Fix 2: Disable IPv6 on Your Device

If your VPN doesn't fully handle IPv6, disabling IPv6 at the OS level removes that leak vector entirely.

**On Windows:**
1. Open Network and Sharing Center
2. Click on your active network connection
3. Click Properties
4. Uncheck "Internet Protocol Version 6 (TCP/IPv6)"
5. Click OK

**On macOS:**
1. Go to System Settings > Network
2. Select your active connection > Details
3. Click the TCP/IP tab
4. Change "Configure IPv6" to "Link-local only" or "Off"

### Fix 3: Remove Static DNS Entries

**On Windows:**
1. Open Network and Sharing Center > Change adapter settings
2. Right-click your active adapter > Properties
3. Select IPv4 > Properties
4. Set both DNS fields to "Obtain DNS server address automatically"
5. Repeat for IPv6

### Fix 4: Disable Windows Smart Multi-Homed Name Resolution

This requires editing Group Policy, which is only available on Windows Pro and Enterprise. For Home editions, disabling the feature requires a registry edit. Alternatively, a properly configured VPN client should handle this for you.

### Fix 5: Update Your VPN Client

Old VPN clients have known leak bugs that have been patched in later versions. Check for updates and reinstall if the problem persists.

## Which VPNs Are Known to Leak?

Free VPNs and lesser-known providers are more likely to have DNS leak issues than established paid providers. In independent tests conducted in 2024–2025:

- **NordVPN, ProtonVPN, Mullvad, and ExpressVPN** consistently pass DNS leak tests across all major platforms.
- **Free browser VPN extensions** (not full VPN clients) frequently fail DNS leak tests because they only proxy HTTPS traffic, not DNS.
- **Hotspot Shield** has had reported DNS leak issues on specific configurations.
- Any VPN that routes DNS through third-party servers (like Google or Cloudflare) rather than their own DNS infrastructure is inherently less private, even without a technical leak.

## Quick Checklist

Before declaring your VPN safe on DNS:

- [ ] Run dnsleaktest.com while connected. DNS servers shown should match your VPN, not your ISP
- [ ] Test on IPv6 too (ipleak.net shows this)
- [ ] Check that no static DNS entries exist on your network adapters
- [ ] Verify your VPN app is the latest version
- [ ] Reconnect to a different VPN server and test again

## Recommendation

For users who want DNS leak protection without manual configuration: **NordVPN** and **ProtonVPN** both handle DNS automatically and have passed independent leak tests consistently. Neither requires you to touch IPv6 settings or Group Policy. Connect, test once to confirm, and you're done.
